beginner.question: What are industrial robots used for?

options.B: To improve productivity, accuracy, safety, and quality in manufacturing

beginner.explanation: Industrial robots improve productivity, accuracy, safety, and quality while reducing human effort and operational costs.

beginner.question: In which industries are robots widely used?

options.B: Automobile, electronics, aerospace, pharmaceuticals, logistics, warehousing, and food processing

beginner.explanation: Robots are widely used in industries such as automobile manufacturing, electronics production, aerospace, pharmaceuticals, logistics, warehousing, and food processing.

beginner.question: How are robot applications broadly classified?

options.B: Material Transfer, Machine Loading/Unloading, Processing, Assembly, Inspection

beginner.explanation: Robot applications are classified into Material Transfer, Machine Loading/Unloading, Processing Operations, Assembly Operations, and Inspection/Quality Control.

beginner.question: What is material transfer in robotics?

options.B: Moving materials from one location to another without changing physical characteristics

beginner.explanation: Material transfer involves moving materials from one location to another without changing the physical characteristics of the material.

beginner.question: What are typical material transfer applications?

options.B: Pick and place, packaging, palletizing, depalletizing

beginner.explanation: Typical material transfer applications include pick and place operations, packaging, palletizing, depalletizing, warehouse automation, and conveyor handling.

beginner.question: What are AMRs and AGVs used for?
beginner.explanation: Modern warehouses use Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) and Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) for material transfer.

beginner.question: What is machine loading and unloading in robotics?

options.B: Automatically placing workpieces into machines and removing finished products

beginner.explanation: Machine loading and unloading robots are used to automatically place workpieces into machines and remove finished products.

beginner.question: With which machines are loading/unloading robots commonly used?

options.B: CNC machines, milling machines, drilling machines, injection molding machines, die casting machines

beginner.explanation: These robots are commonly used with CNC machines, milling machines, drilling machines, injection molding machines, and die casting machines.

beginner.question: What is a benefit of machine loading/unloading robots?

options.B: Higher production rate and reduced cycle time

beginner.explanation: Benefits include higher production rate, reduced cycle time, improved worker safety, and consistent operation.

beginner.question: What are processing operations in robotics?

options.B: Robots directly performing manufacturing tasks on workpieces

beginner.explanation: Processing operations involve robots directly performing manufacturing tasks on workpieces.

beginner.question: Which welding types do robots perform?
beginner.explanation: Robots perform arc welding and spot welding operations.

beginner.question: Where are welding robots commonly used?
beginner.explanation: Welding robots are used in automobile manufacturing, shipbuilding, and heavy engineering.

beginner.question: What are benefits of robotic painting?

options.B: Uniform coating, reduced paint wastage, safe operation in hazardous environments

beginner.explanation: Robotic painting ensures uniform coating, reduced paint wastage, and safe operation in hazardous environments.

beginner.question: Which cutting processes can robots perform?

options.B: Laser cutting, plasma cutting, water-jet cutting

beginner.explanation: Robots perform laser cutting, plasma cutting, and water-jet cutting operations.

beginner.question: Where are grinding and polishing robots used?

options.B: Automotive parts, metal finishing, consumer products

beginner.explanation: Grinding and polishing robots are used in automotive parts, metal finishing, and consumer products.

beginner.question: What are assembly robots used for?

options.B: Joining components together to form a final product

beginner.explanation: Assembly robots join components together to form a final product.

beginner.question: What are typical assembly operations?

options.B: Screw driving, press fitting, PCB assembly, component insertion

beginner.explanation: Typical assembly operations include screw driving, press fitting, PCB assembly, component insertion, and electronic product assembly.

beginner.question: Which robot type is widely used for assembly?
beginner.explanation: SCARA robots are widely used for assembly because of their high speed and repeatability.

beginner.question: What do inspection robots use to evaluate product quality?
beginner.explanation: Inspection robots use sensors and vision systems including cameras, vision sensors, laser scanners, infrared sensors, and force sensors.

beginner.question: What can inspection robots identify?

options.B: Surface defects, dimensional errors, missing components, incorrect assembly

beginner.explanation: Inspection robots can identify surface defects, dimensional errors, missing components, and incorrect assembly.

beginner.question: What is robot programming?

options.B: Creating instructions that control robot motion and operations

beginner.explanation: Robot programming refers to the process of creating instructions that control robot motion and operations.

beginner.question: What does a robot program define?

options.B: Motion sequence, speed, position, tool actions, sensor interactions

beginner.explanation: A robot program defines motion sequence, speed, position, tool actions, and sensor interactions.

beginner.question: What is lead-through programming?

options.B: Operator physically guides the robot through desired positions

beginner.explanation: In lead-through programming, the operator physically guides the robot through desired positions and the robot records the path.

beginner.question: What are applications of lead-through programming?
beginner.explanation: Lead-through programming is used for painting and welding applications.

beginner.question: What is an advantage of lead-through programming?

options.B: Easy to learn and no coding required

beginner.explanation: Advantages of lead-through programming include being easy to learn with no coding required.

beginner.question: What is a disadvantage of lead-through programming?
beginner.explanation: Disadvantages include being time-consuming and having limited flexibility.

beginner.question: What is teach pendant programming?

options.B: Using a handheld device to move the robot and record positions

beginner.explanation: A teach pendant is a handheld device used to move the robot and record positions; this is the most common industrial robot programming method.

beginner.question: What is an advantage of teach pendant programming?
beginner.explanation: Advantages include easy implementation and good accuracy.

beginner.question: What is offline programming?

options.B: Creating programs using simulation software before downloading to robot

beginner.explanation: Offline programming uses simulation software to create programs before downloading them to the robot.

beginner.question: What is an advantage of offline programming?

options.B: No production interruption and faster deployment

beginner.explanation: Advantages include no production interruption and faster deployment.

beginner.question: What does RAIL stand for?
beginner.explanation: RAIL stands for Robot Assembly Language, developed specifically for robotic assembly tasks.

beginner.question: What are features of RAIL language?
beginner.explanation: RAIL features include high-level language, assembly-oriented commands, easy motion specification, and suitability for industrial automation.

beginner.question: What does VAL II stand for?
beginner.explanation: VAL II stands for Variable Assembly Language, developed by Unimation.

beginner.question: Who developed VAL II language?
beginner.explanation: VAL II was developed by Unimation and became one of the earliest successful robot programming languages.

beginner.question: What are features of VAL II?

options.B: Structured programming, motion commands, sensor integration, decision making, error handling

beginner.explanation: VAL II features include structured programming, motion commands, sensor integration, decision making, and error handling.

beginner.question: What is an example VAL II command?
beginner.explanation: Example VAL II commands include MOVE, APPROACH, DEPART, OPEN, and CLOSE.

beginner.question: What is INFORM?

options.B: Proprietary robot programming language developed by Yaskawa for Motoman robots

beginner.explanation: INFORM is the proprietary robot programming language developed by Yaskawa Electric Corporation for its Motoman industrial robots.

beginner.question: What are applications of INFORM language?

options.B: Arc welding, spot welding, material handling, machine tending, painting, assembly

beginner.explanation: INFORM is widely used in arc welding robots, spot welding systems, material handling robots, and assembly automation.

beginner.question: What is URScript?

options.B: Programming language used by Universal Robots for collaborative robots (Cobots)

beginner.explanation: URScript is the programming language used by Universal Robots for collaborative robots (Cobots).

beginner.question: What are features of URScript?

options.B: Simple syntax, supports collaborative robots, easy integration with external devices

beginner.explanation: URScript features simple syntax, supports collaborative robots, easy integration with external devices, and compatibility with machine vision systems.

beginner.question: What is ROS?

options.A: Robot Operating System - an open-source robotics framework

beginner.explanation: ROS (Robot Operating System) is an open-source robotics framework used extensively in research, education, and industrial robotics.

beginner.question: Is ROS actually an operating system?

options.A: Yes, it is a full operating system
options.B: No, it is a middleware platform that provides libraries, tools, and communication frameworks
options.D: No, it is a hardware component

beginner.explanation: ROS is not actually an operating system but a middleware platform that provides libraries, tools, communication frameworks, and software packages for robot development.

beginner.question: What are nodes in ROS?

options.B: Independent software modules performing specific functions

beginner.explanation: Nodes are independent software modules performing specific functions that communicate with each other.

beginner.question: What are topics in ROS?
beginner.explanation: Topics are communication channels used between nodes for data exchange.

beginner.question: What are services in ROS?
beginner.explanation: Services provide a request-response communication mechanism between nodes.

beginner.question: What are applications of ROS?

options.B: Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs), self-driving vehicles, drone systems, humanoid robots, service robots

beginner.explanation: ROS applications include Autonomous Mobile Robots, self-driving vehicles, drone systems, humanoid robots, and service robots.

beginner.question: What is AI-Based Robot Programming?

options.B: Enabling robots to learn, adapt, reason, and make decisions autonomously

beginner.explanation: AI-Based Robot Programming enables robots to learn, adapt, reason, and make decisions autonomously using machine learning, computer vision, and other AI technologies.

beginner.question: What is a Digital Twin?

options.B: A virtual representation of a physical robot that updates in real time

beginner.explanation: A Digital Twin is a virtual representation of a physical robot that continuously receives data from the real robot and updates its virtual model in real time.

beginner.question: What are benefits of Digital Twins?

options.B: Reduced downtime, faster commissioning, lower development cost, improved safety

beginner.explanation: Benefits include reduced downtime, faster commissioning, lower development cost, improved safety, and better system performance.

intermediate.question: A warehouse robot moves 60 meters in 12 seconds. What is its speed?
intermediate.explanation: Speed = Distance ÷ Time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

intermediate.question: A robot loads a CNC machine in 8 seconds and unloads in 7 seconds. What is total handling time?
intermediate.explanation: Total Time = Loading Time + Unloading Time = 8 + 7 = 15 seconds.

intermediate.question: A welding robot completes one weld in 20 seconds. How many welds per hour?
intermediate.explanation: Number of Welds = 3600 ÷ 20 = 180 welds per hour.

intermediate.question: A robot assembles one product every 30 seconds. How many products in 8 hours?
intermediate.explanation: 8 hours = 28800 seconds; Number = 28800 ÷ 30 = 960 products.

intermediate.question: A vision robot inspected 500 products and detected defects correctly in 485. What is inspection accuracy?
intermediate.explanation: Accuracy = (Correct ÷ Total) × 100 = (485 ÷ 500) × 100 = 97%.

intermediate.question: A warehouse robot travels 120 meters in 24 seconds carrying 40 kg at 2 m/s². What is its speed?
intermediate.explanation: Speed = Distance ÷ Time = 120 ÷ 24 = 5 m/s.

intermediate.question: For the same warehouse robot, what force is generated (mass=40kg, a=2 m/s²)?
intermediate.explanation: Force = Mass × Acceleration = 40 × 2 = 80 N.

intermediate.question: An assembly robot assembles 1 component every 15 seconds for 6 hours. How many components?
intermediate.explanation: 6 hours = 21600 seconds; Components = 21600 ÷ 15 = 1440.

intermediate.question: A robot travels 150 meters in 30 seconds. What is its speed?
A robot accelerates a 25 kg object at 3 m/s². What is the force?
intermediate.explanation: Force = 25 × 3 = 75 N.

intermediate.question: A palletizing robot stacks 20 boxes per minute. How many boxes in 2 hours?
intermediate.explanation: 2 hours = 120 minutes; Boxes = 20 × 120 = 2400.

intermediate.question: A pick-and-place robot moves 2000 parts in 1 hour. What is the cycle time per part?
intermediate.explanation: Cycle time = 3600 ÷ 2000 = 1.8 seconds per part.

intermediate.question: A painting robot covers 10 m² per minute. How much area in 4 hours?
intermediate.explanation: 4 hours = 240 minutes; Area = 10 × 240 = 2400 m².

intermediate.question: An inspection robot checks 2 products per second. How many in 30 minutes?
intermediate.explanation: 30 minutes = 1800 seconds; Products = 2 × 1800 = 3600.

intermediate.question: A welding robot has an arc-on time of 85%. What is actual welding time in an hour?
intermediate.explanation: Welding time = 60 × 0.85 = 51 minutes.

intermediate.question: Which robot application involves moving materials without changing their properties?
intermediate.explanation: Material transfer involves moving materials without changing physical characteristics.

intermediate.question: What is a key benefit of using robots for machine loading/unloading?

options.B: Improved machine utilization and reduced idle time

intermediate.explanation: Robots improve machine utilization and reduce idle time, leading to higher production rates.

intermediate.question: Why are SCARA robots preferred for assembly?
intermediate.explanation: SCARA robots are widely used for assembly because of their high speed and repeatability.

intermediate.question: What do inspection robots commonly use for quality evaluation?
intermediate.explanation: Inspection systems commonly use cameras, vision sensors, laser scanners, infrared sensors, and force sensors.

intermediate.question: Which programming method is most common in industrial robots?
intermediate.explanation: Teach pendant programming is the most common industrial robot programming method.

intermediate.question: Which programming method requires simulation software?
intermediate.explanation: Offline programming uses simulation software to create programs before downloading to the robot.

intermediate.question: Which robot language was developed by Unimation?
intermediate.explanation: VAL II (Variable Assembly Language) was developed by Unimation.

intermediate.question: Which robot language was developed specifically for assembly tasks?
intermediate.explanation: RAIL (Robot Assembly Language) was developed specifically for robotic assembly tasks.

intermediate.question: Which robot language is proprietary to Yaskawa Motoman?
intermediate.explanation: INFORM is the proprietary robot programming language developed by Yaskawa for Motoman robots.

intermediate.question: Which robot language is used by Universal Robots for cobots?
intermediate.explanation: URScript is the programming language used by Universal Robots for collaborative robots.

intermediate.question: What does the VAL II command 'APPROACH' do?

options.B: Moves the robot to a position near the target

intermediate.explanation: APPROACH is a VAL II command that moves the robot to a position near the target.

intermediate.question: What does the VAL II command 'DEPART' do?

options.A: Moves away from the current position

intermediate.explanation: DEPART is a VAL II command that moves the robot away from the current position.

intermediate.question: What is an example INFORM command?
intermediate.explanation: MOVJ P001 is an INFORM command that moves the robot to position P001 using joint interpolation.

intermediate.question: What is an example URScript command?
intermediate.explanation: movej([0,-1.57,1.57,0,1.57,0]) is a URScript command that moves robot joints to specified positions.

intermediate.question: What is the key characteristic of a Digital Twin?

options.A: It is a physical copy of the robot
options.B: It receives real-time data from the physical robot

intermediate.explanation: The digital twin continuously receives data from the real robot and updates its virtual model in real time.

intermediate.question: What is an advantage of offline programming?
intermediate.explanation: Offline programming allows program creation without interrupting production.

intermediate.question: What type of AI learning enables robots to learn from trial and error?
intermediate.explanation: Reinforcement learning enables robots to learn autonomously through trial and error.

intermediate.question: Which ROS component defines data structures exchanged between nodes?
intermediate.explanation: Messages are data structures exchanged between nodes in ROS.

intermediate.question: A warehouse robot has 95% uptime. How many minutes does it operate in an 8-hour shift?
intermediate.explanation: Operating time = 480 × 0.95 = 456 minutes.

intermediate.question: A robot cell produces 120 parts per hour. How many parts in 7.5 hours?
intermediate.explanation: Parts = 120 × 7.5 = 900 parts.

intermediate.question: Which application uses robots for screw driving and press fitting?
intermediate.explanation: Assembly operations include screw driving, press fitting, PCB assembly, and component insertion.

intermediate.question: What is a disadvantage of lead-through programming?
intermediate.explanation: Lead-through programming is time-consuming and has limited flexibility for complex paths.

intermediate.question: What is a disadvantage of offline programming?
intermediate.explanation: Offline programming requires specialized software which can be costly.

intermediate.question: Which robot programming method uses simulation before deployment?
intermediate.explanation: Offline programming uses simulation software to create and test programs before deployment.

intermediate.question: What is the primary purpose of AI in robot programming?

options.B: To enable adaptive behavior and autonomous decision making

intermediate.explanation: AI enables robots to learn, adapt, reason, and make decisions autonomously.

intermediate.question: What is the advantage of using Digital Twins before robot deployment?

options.B: Detects collisions and optimizes cycle time virtually
options.C: Requires robot to be built first

intermediate.explanation: Engineers test processes inside a digital twin environment to detect collisions and optimize cycle time before real deployment.

intermediate.question: A robotic painting cell has 92% efficiency. What is actual production time in an 8-hour shift?
intermediate.explanation: Actual time = 8 × 0.92 = 7.36 hours.

intermediate.question: What does ROS provide for robot development?

options.B: Libraries, tools, communication frameworks, and software packages

intermediate.explanation: ROS provides libraries, tools, communication frameworks, and software packages for robot development.

intermediate.question: Which robot language uses joint interpolation command 'MOVJ'?
intermediate.explanation: INFORM uses MOVJ command for joint interpolation movement.

intermediate.question: Which AI technology is used for object recognition in robotics?
intermediate.explanation: Deep Learning and Computer Vision are used for object recognition in robotics.

intermediate.question: A packaging robot packs 25 boxes per minute. How many in 3.5 hours?
intermediate.explanation: 3.5 hours = 210 minutes; Boxes = 25 × 210 = 5250.

intermediate.question: What is a key feature of ROS?
intermediate.explanation: ROS is open-source with a large robotics community supporting multiple programming languages.

intermediate.question: Which robot application uses laser scanners and cameras for quality evaluation?
intermediate.explanation: Inspection robots use cameras, vision sensors, laser scanners, and infrared sensors for quality evaluation.

advanced.question: A robotic work cell has a reliability of 0.95 and availability of 0.98. What is the overall equipment effectiveness (OEE) if quality rate is 0.99?
advanced.explanation: OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality = 0.98 × 0.95 × 0.99 = 0.92169 ≈ 0.92.

advanced.question: A robot cell has mean time between failures (MTBF) of 500 hours and mean time to repair (MTTR) of 10 hours. What is availability?
A welding robot has an arc-on time of 75% and produces 5 welds per minute during arc-on time. What is actual production rate per hour?
advanced.explanation: Arc-on time per hour = 60 × 0.75 = 45 minutes; Welds = 5 × 45 = 225 per hour.

advanced.question: An assembly robot has cycle time of 12 seconds but only 85% efficiency. How many assemblies in 8 hours?
A palletizing robot places 15 boxes per minute with 95% reliability. How many boxes in 6 hours?
advanced.explanation: Theoretical = 15 × 360 = 5400; Actual = 5400 × 0.95 = 5130 boxes.

advanced.question: An inspection robot has 98% accuracy and inspects 3 products per second. How many correct inspections in 2 hours?
advanced.explanation: Total inspections = 3 × 7200 = 21600; Correct = 21600 × 0.98 = 21168.

advanced.question: What is the main difference between VAL II and modern languages like URScript?

options.A: VAL II is object-oriented, URScript is procedural
options.B: VAL II was for mainframe computers, URScript is for collaborative robots

advanced.explanation: VAL II was developed for early industrial robots on mainframe computers, while URScript is designed for modern collaborative robots.

advanced.question: In ROS, what is the difference between a topic and a service?

options.A: Topics are one-way continuous streams; services are request-response
options.B: Services are continuous; topics are one-time

advanced.explanation: Topics provide continuous asynchronous data streams (publish-subscribe), while services provide synchronous request-response communication.

advanced.question: What is the advantage of using Digital Twins for predictive maintenance?

options.B: Predicts failures before they occur using real-time data

advanced.explanation: Digital twins enable predictive maintenance by analyzing real-time data to predict failures before they occur.

advanced.question: A work cell has a target throughput of 500 parts/hour. Actual throughput is 450 parts/hour. What is the performance efficiency?
advanced.explanation: Performance = Actual/Target = 450/500 = 0.9 = 90%.

advanced.question: A robot cell produces 1000 good parts out of 1100 total parts. What is the quality rate?
What is the key advantage of AI-based robot programming over traditional programming?

options.B: Adaptive behavior and learning from experience

advanced.explanation: AI enables adaptive behavior and learning from experience, unlike traditional pre-programmed robots.

advanced.question: A dual-arm collaborative robot has cycle time of 25 seconds for assembly but one arm fails 5% of the time. What is effective cycle time?
advanced.explanation: Reliability factor = 1/(1-0.05) = 1/0.95 = 1.0526; Effective cycle = 25 × 1.0526 = 26.315 s.

advanced.question: What is the primary difference between RAIL and VAL II?

options.A: RAIL is for assembly, VAL II is more general-purpose
options.B: RAIL is older than VAL II
options.D: VAL II is only for painting

advanced.explanation: RAIL was specifically developed for assembly tasks, while VAL II was a more general-purpose language for various industrial applications.

advanced.question: In ROS, what is the purpose of the ROS Master?

options.B: Manages node registration and topic/service discovery

advanced.explanation: The ROS Master manages node registration, topic, and service discovery, enabling nodes to find each other.

advanced.question: A flexible manufacturing cell uses 6 robots with individual availability of 0.97. What is system availability?
advanced.explanation: System availability = (0.97)^6 = 0.833 ≈ 0.83. Closest is 0.82.

advanced.question: What is the role of the ROS parameter server?

options.A: Stores runtime parameters accessible to all nodes

advanced.explanation: The parameter server stores configuration parameters that can be accessed by all nodes in the ROS system.

advanced.question: What is the purpose of the Gazebo simulator in ROS?
advanced.explanation: Gazebo is a 3D robotics simulator integrated with ROS that provides physics simulation for robot testing.

advanced.question: An automated warehouse uses 10 AGVs with mean time between failures of 200 hours each. What is system MTBF?
advanced.explanation: For series system, failure rate adds. λ_total = 10 × (1/200) = 0.05 failures/hour; MTBF = 1/0.05 = 20 hours.

advanced.question: A robotic processing cell has a design capacity of 800 units/day but achieves 680 units/day due to downtime. What is utilization?
advanced.explanation: Utilization = Actual/Design = 680/800 = 0.85 = 85%.

advanced.question: What is the main advantage of URScript over INFORM for certain applications?

options.B: URScript is designed for collaborative robots with simpler syntax

advanced.explanation: URScript is specifically designed for collaborative robots with simpler syntax and easier integration.

advanced.question: A robotic assembly line has 5 stations in series. Each station has 98% reliability. What is overall line reliability?
What is a key limitation of traditional robot programming languages compared to ROS?

options.B: They are proprietary with limited reusability across robot brands

advanced.explanation: Traditional languages like INFORM, VAL II are proprietary to specific robot brands, limiting code reusability across different robots.

advanced.question: A pick-and-place robot has a mean cycle time of 3 seconds with standard deviation 0.2 seconds. What is the cycle time at 99.7% confidence (3σ)?
advanced.explanation: 3σ range = mean ± 3σ = 3 ± 0.6 = 2.4 to 3.6 seconds.

advanced.question: What is the primary advantage of using ROS over proprietary languages for research?

options.B: Open-source with large community and reusable packages

advanced.explanation: ROS is open-source with a large community and many reusable packages, making it ideal for research and development.

advanced.question: A machine tending robot has loading time 12 s, unloading time 10 s, machine process time 120 s. What is the cycle time?
advanced.explanation: Cycle time = Load + Unload + Process = 12 + 10 + 120 = 142 seconds.

advanced.question: For the same robot, what is the robot utilization percentage?
advanced.explanation: Robot utilization = (Load+Unload)/Total cycle = 22/142 = 0.155 = 15.5%.

advanced.question: What is a key feature of Digital Twins for Industry 4.0?

options.B: Real-time synchronization with physical assets for optimization

advanced.explanation: Digital twins provide real-time synchronization with physical assets, enabling continuous optimization and predictive analytics.

advanced.question: A robotic work cell has a first-pass yield of 95% and rework rate of 3%. What is final quality rate after rework?
advanced.explanation: If 3% are reworked and rework has 100% success, final yield = 95% + 3% = 98%.

advanced.question: What is the main difference between lead-through and teach pendant programming?

options.A: Lead-through requires physical guidance, teach pendant uses a handheld device

advanced.explanation: Lead-through requires physically moving the robot, while teach pendant uses a handheld device to jog the robot and record positions.

advanced.question: A dual-robot collaborative cell has Robot A reliability 0.96, Robot B reliability 0.94. What is system reliability if both must work?
advanced.explanation: System reliability = 0.96 × 0.94 = 0.9024 ≈ 0.90.

advanced.question: What is the purpose of the 'TF' (Transform) library in ROS?
advanced.explanation: The TF library in ROS tracks multiple coordinate frames over time, enabling transformations between different robot components.

advanced.question: A painting robot has a booth cleaning downtime of 30 minutes every 8-hour shift. What is availability?
advanced.explanation: Operating time = 480 - 30 = 450 minutes; Availability = 450/480 = 0.9375 = 93.75%.

advanced.question: What is the key innovation of UR robots (Universal Robots) in programming?

options.B: They introduced intuitive drag-to-teach programming for cobots

advanced.explanation: Universal Robots introduced intuitive drag-to-teach programming, making collaborative robots accessible to non-programmers.

advanced.question: A robotic inspection system has sensitivity of 99% and specificity of 98%. What is the overall accuracy if defect rate is 5%?
advanced.explanation: Overall accuracy = (Sensitivity × Defect rate) + (Specificity × Good rate) = (0.99×0.05)+(0.98×0.95)=0.0495+0.931=0.9805=98.05%.

advanced.question: What is the main advantage of using Actionlib in ROS over services?

options.B: Actions provide feedback and can be preempted for long-running tasks

advanced.explanation: Actionlib provides feedback during task execution and allows preemption, ideal for long-running tasks like robot navigation.

advanced.question: A pick-and-place robot has a cycle time of 2.5 seconds but jam occurs every 1000 cycles taking 60 seconds to clear. What is effective cycle time?
advanced.explanation: Per 1000 cycles: total time = (1000×2.5)+60 = 2560 seconds; Average = 2560/1000 = 2.56 seconds.

advanced.question: What is the primary purpose of the ROS Navigation Stack?

options.B: Mobile robot localization and path planning

advanced.explanation: The ROS Navigation Stack provides localization, path planning, and obstacle avoidance for mobile robots.

advanced.question: An assembly robot has a design rate of 1200 units/hour but produces 1080 units/hour. What is the speed efficiency?
advanced.explanation: Speed efficiency = 1080/1200 = 0.9 = 90%.

advanced.question: What is the main advantage of using RVIZ in ROS?

options.B: 3D visualization of robot data and sensor information

advanced.explanation: RVIZ is a 3D visualization tool in ROS for displaying robot models, sensor data, and navigation information.

advanced.question: A robotic cell has a total of 480 minutes available, 30 minutes downtime, and produces 450 good parts. Design rate is 1 part/minute. What is OEE?
advanced.explanation: Availability=450/480=0.9375, Performance=450/450=1.0, Quality=450/450=1.0. OEE=0.9375×1×1=93.75%. Wait, design rate 1/min, actual production 450 parts in 450 min operating, so performance=450/450=1. Actually OEE=(450/480)=0.9375? Let me recalc properly. Actually OEE = (Good parts × Ideal cycle time)/Planned time = (450×1)/480=0.9375. But with quality and performance factors? Better: Availability=450/480=0.9375, Performance=1, Quality=1, OEE=93.75%.

advanced.question: What is the main difference between ROS 1 and ROS 2?

options.A: ROS 2 has real-time support and improved security

advanced.explanation: ROS 2 adds real-time support, improved security, better multi-robot communication, and eliminates the single master architecture.

advanced.question: A welding robot has electrode change downtime of 5 minutes every 2 hours. What is the effective availability?
advanced.explanation: In 120 minutes, downtime=5 minutes. Availability = (120-5)/120 = 115/120 = 0.9583 = 95.8%.

advanced.question: What is the purpose of MoveIt in ROS?

options.B: Robot manipulator motion planning and kinematics

advanced.explanation: MoveIt is a ROS package for robot manipulator motion planning, kinematics, collision checking, and trajectory execution.

advanced.question: An autonomous mobile robot has a battery runtime of 6 hours and charging time of 1 hour. What is maximum theoretical utilization?
advanced.explanation: Utilization = Runtime/(Runtime+Charge)=6/(6+1)=6/7=0.857=85.7%.

advanced.question: What is the key advantage of using AI-based visual inspection over traditional rule-based inspection?

options.B: Can learn and adapt to new defect types

advanced.explanation: AI-based visual inspection can learn from data and adapt to new defect types without explicit reprogramming.

advanced.question: A robotic packaging line has 3 parallel robots each with reliability 0.95. What is system reliability if at least 2 must work?
advanced.explanation: P(2 working)=3×(0.95²×0.05)=0.135, P(3 working)=0.857, Total=0.992 (using binomial).

advanced.question: What is the main advantage of using Docker containers with ROS?

options.B: Provides reproducible environments for ROS development

advanced.explanation: Docker containers provide reproducible, isolated environments for ROS development and deployment.

advanced.question: A multi-robot system has 8 robots with individual MTBF of 1000 hours. What is system MTBF for series configuration?
advanced.explanation: λ_total = 8/1000 = 0.008 failures/hour; MTBF = 1/0.008 = 125 hours.
